network.overall_strategy: L-Alanine is synthesized through a convergent prebiotic network centered on pyruvate as the primary metabolic hub. Four major routes supply pyruvate: (1) Fischer-Tropsch-type CO2 fixation on native iron/awaruite/magnetite at hydrothermal vents (Preiner et al., 2020), (2) iron-catalyzed decarboxylation of oxaloacetate (Muchowska et al., 2019), (3) cyanosulfidic decarboxylation of oxaloacetate (Ritson, 2021), and (4) cyanosulfidic oxidation of lactic acid (Ritson, 2021). Pyruvate is then converted to alanine via iron-promoted reductive amination with hydroxylamine (Muchowska et al., 2019) or with ammonia. A parallel surface pathway converts 2-aminopropanenitrile to alanine via nitrile hydrolysis (Patel et al., 2015), with the aminonitrile itself produced via Strecker synthesis from acetaldehyde, ammonia, and HCN. Acetaldehyde is also accessible from pyruvate thermal decarboxylation, linking hydrothermal and surface routes. Additionally, hydrothermal reduction of L-serine provides a direct amino acid interconversion route (Aubrey et al., 2008). The network exhibits strong environmental interconnection: hydrothermal CO2 fixation feeds surface Strecker chemistry via the acetaldehyde bridge, while surface cyanosulfidic products feed hydrothermal reductive amination via pyruvate. Final biochemical assembly uses alanine for peptide formation on clay minerals.

reactions.name: Fischer-Tropsch-type CO2 fixation to pyruvate

conditions.other: Stoichiometry: 3 CO₂ + 5 H₂ → pyruvate + 3 H₂O; H₂ from serpentinization; tested under three catalyst combinations: (a) Fe⁰ + awaruite, (b) Fe⁰ + magnetite, (c) magnetite alone

reactions.mechanism: H₂-dependent CO₂ reduction on native metal surfaces analogous to Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis. Iron and nickel-iron alloy (awaruite) surfaces activate H₂ and reduce CO₂ stepwise through C1 intermediates (formate, formaldehyde, CO) to C2 (acetate) and then C3 (pyruvate) products. Magnetite provides an oxide surface facilitating CO₂ adsorption and reduction.
reactions.reasoning: Preiner, Igarashi, Muchowska, Yu, Varma, Kleinermanns, Nobu, Kamagata, Tüysüz, Moran, and Martin (2020, Nature Ecology & Evolution, DOI: 10.1038/s41559-020-1125-6) demonstrated that CO₂ and H₂ react on native iron, awaruite, and magnetite at 100°C to produce pyruvate and other metabolic intermediates. This provides a purely geochemical route from the simplest serpentinization-derived feedstocks to the central metabolic hub molecule pyruvate.

reactions.name: Iron-promoted reductive amination of pyruvate with hydroxylamine

conditions.other: Stoichiometry: pyruvate + NH₂OH → alanine + 2 H₂O (net); mild hydrothermal conditions at alkaline vent mixing zones

reactions.mechanism: Iron-promoted reductive amination: hydroxylamine acts as the nitrogen donor, and iron species provide the reducing equivalents to drive the transamination. The α-keto acid (pyruvate) is converted to the corresponding α-amino acid (alanine). Fe⁰ serves as the bulk reductant while Fe²⁺ acts as Lewis acid co-catalyst.
reactions.reasoning: Muchowska, Varma, and Moran (2019, Nature, DOI: 10.1038/s41586-019-1151-1) demonstrated that metallic iron and ferrous iron at 70°C in water promote interconversion of α-keto acids and α-amino acids via reductive amination with hydroxylamine. Specifically, pyruvate → alanine was demonstrated as part of an interconnected geochemical analog of core biological metabolism.

reactions.name: Iron-catalyzed decarboxylation of oxaloacetate to pyruvate (hydrothermal)

conditions.other: β-decarboxylation; thermodynamically favorable at mild temperatures

reactions.mechanism: β-decarboxylation of oxaloacetate catalyzed by Fe²⁺ ions. Iron(II) acts as a Lewis acid to coordinate the β-keto acid and facilitate CO₂ loss, yielding pyruvate and CO₂. This reaction is thermodynamically favorable and proceeds readily under mild hydrothermal conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Muchowska, Varma, and Moran (2019, Nature, DOI: 10.1038/s41586-019-1151-1) demonstrated Fe²⁺-catalyzed decarboxylation of oxaloacetate to pyruvate at 70°C as part of a simple geochemical analog of the reductive TCA cycle. This provides a direct route from C4 dicarboxylic acids to the C3 pyruvate hub.

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic decarboxylation of oxaloacetate to pyruvate (surface)

conditions.catalyst: sulfide minerals (stibnite, molybdenite) under cyanosulfidic conditions
conditions.other: Reaction complete within ~1 hour; part of cyanosulfidic Krebs cycle analog; UV exposure may assist

reactions.mechanism: Decarboxylation of oxaloacetate under cyanosulfidic conditions in surface evaporitic pools. Sulfide mineral surfaces stabilize the transition state and sulfur-containing species in solution act as electron shuttles, facilitating the decarboxylation. This parallels the biological Krebs cycle decarboxylation but occurs abiotically.
reactions.reasoning: Ritson (2021, Science Advances, DOI: 10.1126/sciadv.abh3981) demonstrated that a cyanosulfidic reaction network can produce the intermediates of the Krebs (TCA) cycle abiotically, including the decarboxylation of oxaloacetate to pyruvate under surface cyanosulfidic conditions. Sulfide minerals are specified as agents consistent with the cyanosulfidic framework of Sutherland and co-workers.

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic oxidation of lactic acid to pyruvate

conditions.other: Sulfur species act as oxidants for the α-hydroxy to α-keto conversion

reactions.mechanism: Oxidation of the α-hydroxy group of lactic acid to a ketone, converting lactate to pyruvate. Under cyanosulfidic conditions, sulfur species (thiosulfate, sulfite, or polysulfide) serve as oxidants, accepting electrons from the hydroxyl group. The sulfide mineral surface provides a catalytic scaffold.
reactions.reasoning: Ritson (2021, Science Advances, DOI: 10.1126/sciadv.abh3981) demonstrated the oxidation of lactic acid to pyruvate under cyanosulfidic conditions as part of a prebiotic analog of the Krebs cycle. This provides a surface-environment route to the pyruvate hub from the more reduced C3 intermediate.

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic nitrile hydrolysis of 2-aminopropanenitrile to alanine

conditions.catalyst: montmorillonite clay (concentrates organics and catalyzes hydrolysis)
conditions.other: Stoichiometry: 2-aminopropanenitrile + 2 H₂O → alanine + NH₃; part of broader cyanosulfidic protometabolism; wet-dry cycling assists

reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic addition of water to the nitrile group of 2-aminopropanenitrile, forming an amide intermediate (2-aminopropanamide), followed by further hydrolysis of the amide to the carboxylic acid (alanine) with release of ammonia. Montmorillonite clay surfaces concentrate the aminonitrile and lower the activation barrier for hydrolysis.
reactions.reasoning: Patel, Percivalle, Ritson, Duffy, and Sutherland (2015, Nature Chemistry, DOI: 10.1038/nchem.2202) demonstrated that 2-aminopropanenitrile is formed via cyanosulfidic Strecker-like chemistry and hydrolyzes to alanine in aqueous conditions. This is part of the unified cyanosulfidic chemistry that produces precursors to RNA, proteins, and lipids. Montmorillonite is included as the mineral agent consistent with its documented role in concentrating and catalyzing organic transformations in surface pools.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal reductive dehydroxylation of L-serine to L-alanine

conditions.catalyst: iron sulfide minerals (pyrite, mackinawite); H₂ from serpentinization
conditions.other: Elevated temperature and pressure characteristic of submarine hydrothermal vents; H₂ abundantly available from serpentinization

reactions.mechanism: Reductive dehydroxylation: the hydroxymethyl group (-CH₂OH) of serine is reduced to a methyl group (-CH₃), converting serine to alanine. Molecular hydrogen serves as the reductant, activated on iron sulfide mineral surfaces. The reaction proceeds through a dehydration intermediate followed by hydrogenation.
reactions.reasoning: Aubrey, Cleaves, and Bada (2008, Origins of Life and Evolution of Biospheres, DOI: 10.1007/s11084-008-9153-2) demonstrated that L-serine can be reduced to L-alanine by molecular hydrogen under simulated submarine hydrothermal vent conditions. Iron sulfide minerals (pyrite, mackinawite) are specified as agents given their ubiquity in hydrothermal systems and established role as catalytic surfaces for reductive transformations.

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis of 2-aminopropanenitrile from acetaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: montmorillonite clay concentrates reactants; TiO₂ may photocatalyze imine formation
conditions.other: Classical Strecker synthesis: aldehyde + ammonia + HCN → α-aminonitrile; pH ~7-9 optimal; evaporitic pool conditions concentrate precursors

reactions.mechanism: Three-component Strecker synthesis. Step 1: Acetaldehyde condenses with ammonia to form an imine (ethylideneamine, CH₃CH=NH). Step 2: Hydrogen cyanide adds nucleophilically to the imine carbon, forming the α-aminonitrile (2-aminopropanenitrile). Montmorillonite clay adsorbs and concentrates all three reactants, promoting the multi-component reaction.
reactions.reasoning: The Strecker synthesis is the classical prebiotic route to α-aminonitriles and has been demonstrated extensively since Miller's original work. Patel et al. (2015, Nature Chemistry, DOI: 10.1038/nchem.2202) incorporated Strecker-like chemistry within their cyanosulfidic framework to produce 2-aminopropanenitrile from acetaldehyde, ammonia, and HCN. Montmorillonite is well-documented as a catalyst for multi-component prebiotic reactions in surface environments.

reactions.name: Thermal decarboxylation of pyruvate to acetaldehyde

conditions.other: Non-oxidative decarboxylation; thermally driven; competes with pyruvate utilization pathways; rate-limiting step is pyruvate accumulation vs. decomposition

reactions.mechanism: Thermal non-oxidative decarboxylation of pyruvate (α-keto acid) to acetaldehyde and CO₂. At elevated hydrothermal temperatures, the β-keto acid moiety of pyruvate readily loses CO₂ through a concerted or stepwise mechanism facilitated by mineral surface adsorption. Magnetite provides Lewis acid sites that coordinate the keto group.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate thermal instability and decarboxylation to acetaldehyde + CO₂ at elevated temperatures is well documented and specifically noted as a key challenge in the open questions section of the literature (see Preiner et al., 2020 and Muchowska et al., 2019). This reaction is critical for the network because it bridges the hydrothermal pyruvate pool to the surface Strecker synthesis pathway by providing acetaldehyde as a C2 building block. Magnetite is specified as the mineral agent consistent with its abundance in hydrothermal systems.

reactions.name: Reductive amination of pyruvate with ammonia (iron-promoted)

conditions.other: Analogous to rxn_002 but with NH₃ as nitrogen donor instead of NH₂OH; H₂ provides additional reducing equivalents; stoichiometry: pyruvate + NH₃ + H₂ → alanine + H₂O

reactions.mechanism: Iron-promoted reductive amination using ammonia as the nitrogen donor instead of hydroxylamine. Pyruvate first condenses with ammonia to form an imine (Schiff base), which is then reduced by Fe⁰/H₂ to yield the amine (alanine). This is the classical reductive amination mechanism. Iron provides reducing equivalents and activates H₂ for the reduction step.
reactions.reasoning: While Muchowska, Varma, and Moran (2019, Nature, DOI: 10.1038/s41586-019-1151-1) specifically demonstrated hydroxylamine as the nitrogen donor, reductive amination of α-keto acids with ammonia on iron surfaces is a well-established prebiotic pathway and is the more classical version of this transformation. Ammonia was far more abundant than hydroxylamine on the early Earth, making this a prebiotically important alternative. The iron-catalyzed conditions and temperature are extrapolated from the Muchowska et al. system.

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic formation of lactic acid from acetaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: sulfide minerals; UV photocatalysis on TiO₂
conditions.other: HCN acts as cyanide source for cyanohydrin formation; hydrolysis yields α-hydroxy acid; part of cyanosulfidic protometabolism

reactions.mechanism: Cyanohydrin reaction: HCN adds to acetaldehyde to form lactonitrile (2-hydroxypropanenitrile, CH₃CH(OH)CN). Subsequent hydrolysis of the nitrile group yields lactic acid (2-hydroxypropanoic acid). This is the hydroxy-acid analog of the Strecker synthesis. TiO₂ photocatalysis and sulfide mineral surfaces facilitate the transformation in surface pool environments.
reactions.reasoning: This reaction connects the acetaldehyde hub to the lactic acid intermediate described by Ritson (2021, Science Advances, DOI: 10.1126/sciadv.abh3981). The cyanohydrin pathway to α-hydroxy acids is a well-known prebiotic route parallel to the Strecker amino acid synthesis and has been described within the broader cyanosulfidic chemistry framework of Patel et al. (2015, Nature Chemistry, DOI: 10.1038/nchem.2202). This reaction is necessary to complete the network by providing a source for lactic acid.

reactions.name: Peptide bond formation from alanine on clay (wet-dry cycling)

conditions.other: Wet-dry cycling: concentration during drying drives condensation; rehydration allows reorganization; multiple cycles increase chain length; stoichiometry: 2 Ala → Ala-Ala + H₂O

reactions.mechanism: Condensation polymerization of amino acids on clay mineral surfaces during wet-dry cycling. During the drying phase, water activity drops and amino acids concentrate on the clay interlayer surfaces, driving thermodynamically unfavorable peptide bond formation. Montmorillonite acts as both adsorbent and catalyst, orienting amino acids for condensation. Repeated cycles increase oligopeptide yield and length.
reactions.reasoning: Peptide bond formation via wet-dry cycling on montmorillonite clay is a well-established prebiotic chemistry pathway. This reaction represents the downstream biochemical utilization of L-alanine produced by the network, connecting the amino acid pool to proto-protein assembly. While not a pathway TO alanine, this reaction completes the chemical narrative from simple molecules through alanine to biologically relevant macromolecules.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal Fischer-Tropsch to pyruvate to alanine (hydroxylamine)
pathways.description: CO₂ and H₂ from serpentinization are fixed to pyruvate on native iron/awaruite/magnetite surfaces at 100°C, then pyruvate undergoes iron-promoted reductive amination with hydroxylamine at 70°C to yield alanine. Entirely hydrothermal pathway.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal oxaloacetate decarboxylation to pyruvate to alanine (hydroxylamine)
pathways.description: Oxaloacetate undergoes Fe²⁺-catalyzed β-decarboxylation to pyruvate at 70°C in hydrothermal vents. Pyruvate then undergoes iron-promoted reductive amination with hydroxylamine to yield alanine. Both steps occur under hydrothermal conditions.

pathways.name: Surface cyanosulfidic oxaloacetate decarboxylation to pyruvate, then hydrothermal reductive amination (hydroxylamine)
pathways.description: Oxaloacetate decarboxylates to pyruvate under cyanosulfidic conditions in surface pools (Ritson, 2021). Pyruvate is then transported to hydrothermal environments where it undergoes iron-promoted reductive amination with hydroxylamine to yield alanine. Cross-environment pathway: Surface → Hydrothermal.

pathways.name: Surface lactic acid oxidation to pyruvate, then hydrothermal reductive amination (hydroxylamine)
pathways.description: Lactic acid is oxidized to pyruvate by cyanosulfidic oxidants in surface pools (Ritson, 2021). Pyruvate is transported to hydrothermal environments for iron-promoted reductive amination with hydroxylamine. Cross-environment: Surface → Hydrothermal.

pathways.name: Surface cyanosulfidic nitrile hydrolysis (direct aminonitrile route)
pathways.description: 2-Aminopropanenitrile, produced by cyanosulfidic Strecker-like chemistry, hydrolyzes in aqueous surface pools to yield alanine and ammonia. Entirely surface pathway, single-step final conversion.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal serine reduction to alanine
pathways.description: L-Serine undergoes reductive dehydroxylation by molecular hydrogen at submarine hydrothermal vents to yield L-alanine. H₂ is abundant from serpentinization. Single-step hydrothermal conversion.

pathways.name: Surface Strecker synthesis to aminonitrile to alanine
pathways.description: Acetaldehyde, ammonia, and HCN undergo Strecker synthesis in surface pools to form 2-aminopropanenitrile, which then hydrolyzes to alanine. Entirely surface pathway through the classical aminonitrile intermediate.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal CO₂ fixation → pyruvate → acetaldehyde → Strecker → alanine (cross-environment)
pathways.description: CO₂ and H₂ are fixed to pyruvate at hydrothermal vents (Preiner et al., 2020). Pyruvate thermally decarboxylates to acetaldehyde at elevated temperatures. Acetaldehyde is transported to surface environments where it undergoes Strecker synthesis with NH₃ and HCN to form 2-aminopropanenitrile, which hydrolyzes to alanine. Major cross-environment pathway: Hydrothermal → Hydrothermal → Surface → Surface.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal oxaloacetate → pyruvate → acetaldehyde → Strecker → alanine
pathways.description: Oxaloacetate decarboxylates to pyruvate via Fe²⁺ catalysis, pyruvate further decarboxylates to acetaldehyde at hydrothermal temperatures, then acetaldehyde enters the surface Strecker synthesis pathway to produce 2-aminopropanenitrile and finally alanine via hydrolysis. Cross-environment: Hydrothermal → Surface.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal Fischer-Tropsch to pyruvate to alanine (ammonia reductive amination)
pathways.description: CO₂ and H₂ are fixed to pyruvate on iron/awaruite/magnetite at 100°C, then pyruvate undergoes reductive amination with ammonia (rather than hydroxylamine) using Fe⁰ and H₂ as reductants. Entirely hydrothermal pathway using the more abundant nitrogen source.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Strongest pathway: directly supported by Preiner et al. (2020) for CO2 fixation and Muchowska et al. (2019) for reductive amination. Both steps operate in the hydrothermal environment with iron-based catalysts. Mineral agents (Fe⁰, awaruite, magnetite) well-specified. Minor concern: assumes hydroxylamine availability, acknowledged as an open challenge in the literature.
suggestions: Explicitly specify mineral catalysts (Fe⁰/awaruite/magnetite) in rxn_001 description and (Fe⁰/Fe²⁺) in rxn_002 description for full clarity

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Both reactions well-supported by Muchowska et al. (2019). Coherent hydrothermal environment. Main weakness: oxaloacetate (mol_007) has no upstream synthesis specified in the network, making it a dangling precursor. Fe²⁺ catalysis for decarboxylation is well-documented.
suggestions: Add an upstream reaction producing oxaloacetate (e.g., from succinate or fumarate via iron-promoted carboxylation as in Muchowska et al.)

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Cross-environment pathway (Surface → Hydrothermal) well-justified. Cyanosulfidic decarboxylation (Ritson 2021) feeds hydrothermal amination (Muchowska 2019). Same oxaloacetate sourcing issue as P2. Surface reaction could benefit from more explicit specification of cyanosulfidic mineral/UV conditions.
suggestions: Specify cyanosulfidic conditions (e.g., cupric cyanide, UV, H2S presence) for rxn_004

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Surface cyanosulfidic oxidation of lactic acid to pyruvate (Ritson 2021), then hydrothermal reductive amination. Cross-environment flow is well-motivated. Lactic acid can be sourced from rxn_011, adding depth. The oxidation mechanism involving sulfur species as oxidants could be more explicitly stated.
suggestions: Explicitly state the sulfur-based oxidant (e.g., cyanosulfidic S-species) in rxn_005
Connect to rxn_011 as upstream supplier

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Single-step nitrile hydrolysis well-supported by Patel et al. (2015). However, listed as only 1 step, which omits the upstream Strecker synthesis (rxn_008) needed to produce 2-aminopropanenitrile. As a standalone step it is valid, but pathway coherence is reduced without including the precursor synthesis. Effectively a sub-pathway of P7.
suggestions: Combine with rxn_008 (Strecker) to form a more complete pathway as in P7

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Hydrothermal serine reduction supported by Aubrey et al. (2008). Valid chemistry under submarine hydrothermal conditions with H2 from serpentinization. Main weakness: L-serine (mol_011) has no upstream synthesis in the network, creating a dangling precursor. Also, the mechanism of reductive dehydroxylation under hydrothermal conditions could be more detailed.
suggestions: Add upstream synthesis of L-serine (e.g., from glycine + formaldehyde, or via Strecker from glycolaldehyde)
Specify temperature and pressure conditions more precisely

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Classic Strecker synthesis to aminonitrile, then hydrolysis to alanine. Both steps in the surface environment are well-justified under Patel et al. (2015) cyanosulfidic framework. Acetaldehyde, ammonia, and HCN are all prebiotically plausible starting materials. Surface mineral catalysts not specified for Strecker step—this is acceptable since Strecker is homogeneous, but UV or mineral facilitation could strengthen it.
suggestions: Note that Strecker synthesis can proceed without mineral catalysts in solution
Consider specifying UV conditions for surface chemistry

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Showcase cross-environment pathway demonstrating the network's interconnection strength. CO2 fixation (hydrothermal, Preiner 2020) → pyruvate → thermal decarboxylation to acetaldehyde (hydrothermal) → Strecker synthesis (surface) → hydrolysis to alanine (surface). The environmental transition from hydrothermal to surface is geologically plausible (vent effluent reaching surface pools). The pyruvate-to-acetaldehyde bridge (rxn_009) cleverly addresses pyruvate instability as noted in the literature while connecting environments. All four reactions have literature support.
suggestions: Quantify the yield losses at each step to assess overall flux viability
Note that pyruvate thermal decarboxylation is normally a degradation issue—here repurposed constructively

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Similar to P8 but starts from oxaloacetate decarboxylation. Same cross-environment strengths. Weakened by the lack of upstream oxaloacetate synthesis. Otherwise chemically sound through all four steps.

CO2 fixation to pyruvate followed by reductive amination with ammonia (instead of hydroxylamine). The ammonia + H2 variant (rxn_010) is less directly supported by Muchowska et al. (2019), who specifically used hydroxylamine as the nitrogen donor. However, reductive amination of alpha-keto acids with ammonia is well-known in organic chemistry and has been discussed in prebiotic contexts. The requirement for both H2 and NH3 plus iron at hydrothermal conditions is plausible but less experimentally validated than the hydroxylamine route.
suggestions: Cite specific experimental evidence for ammonia-based reductive amination under prebiotic conditions
Consider whether FeS-mediated reductive amination with NH3 has been demonstrated

evaluation.network_feedback: This is an excellent prebiotic synthesis network for L-alanine that closely follows the state of the art in origins-of-life chemistry. The network demonstrates strong alignment with five major published studies (Preiner 2020, Muchowska 2019, Patel 2015, Ritson 2021, Aubrey 2008), and the overall strategy correctly identifies pyruvate as the central metabolic hub. Key strengths: (1) Multiple convergent routes to both pyruvate and alanine provide robustness. (2) The acetaldehyde bridge (rxn_009) creatively connects hydrothermal CO2 fixation to surface Strecker chemistry, turning the known instability of pyruvate into a constructive cross-environment link. (3) Hub molecules (pyruvate degree ~7, acetaldehyde degree 3, ammonia degree 3) are well-connected. (4) Environmental flow is well-motivated: Hydrothermal → Surface → Biochemical is clearly traced. (5) Literature citations are appropriate and accurately represented. Minor weaknesses: (1) Oxaloacetate (mol_007), L-serine (mol_011), and hydroxylamine (mol_005) lack upstream synthesis routes in the network, creating dangling precursors. (2) rxn_010 (ammonia reductive amination) has weaker direct experimental support than the hydroxylamine variant. (3) rxn_011 (lactic acid from acetaldehyde + HCN) collapses a multi-step process into one reaction. (4) Some surface reactions lack explicit mineral or UV specifications. Despite these issues, the network is well above threshold for approval.
